data.title: China accuses Philippines of 'playing with fire' on Taiwan
data.description:
China accused the Philippines on Friday of "playing with fire" after President Ferdinand Marcos Jr said the Southeast Asian nation would be drawn into any conflict between China and the United States over Taiwan.

It was responding to remarks by Marcos during a state visit to India that the Philippines' closeness to Taiwan and the large Filipino community there would make involvement necessary in such a conflict.

"'Geographical proximity' and 'large overseas populations' are not excuses for a country to interfere in the internal affairs of others," China's foreign ministry said in a statement.

"We urge the Philippines to earnestly adhere to the one-China principle ... and refrain from playing with fire on issues concerning China's core interests."

Marcos' remarks came amid heightened tension between Beijing and Manila over territorial disputes in the busy waterway of the South China Sea.

Both countries have traded accusations of aggressive manoeuvres and sovereignty violations there, prompting the United States to reaffirm its commitment to defend the Philippines.

Beijing views democratically-governed Taiwan as its territory, a claim Taipei rejects.

The Philippine embassy in Beijing did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

On Wednesday, Marcos told Indian media outlet Firstpost, "If there is an all-out war, then we will be drawn into it."

He added, "There are many, many Filipino nationals in Taiwan and that would be immediately a humanitarian problem.

"We will have to go in there, find a way to go in there, and find a way to bring our people home."

China said such arguments "not only violate international law and the ASEAN charter, but also undermine regional peace and stability and the fundamental interests of (the Philippines') own people."

data.title: Silver Heads for Strong Weekly Gain
data.description:
Silver traded above $38 per ounce on Friday and was on track to rise about 3% for the week, as expectations for US interest rate cuts and uncertainty from new tariffs drove demand for precious metals.

Higher-than-expected weekly jobless claims, coupled with last week’s weaker-than-expected nonfarm payrolls report, prompted traders to price in a Federal Reserve rate cut in September, with another possible move in December.

In political developments, President Donald Trump nominated Stephen Miran, chair of the Council of Economic Advisors, to replace Adriana Kugler on the Federal Reserve Board of Governors.

Markets also weighed reports that Fed Governor Christopher Waller, viewed as more dovish, could be Trump’s pick to lead the central bank, reinforcing expectations of easier policy.

On the trade front, Trump’s sweeping retaliatory tariffs took effect on Thursday, imposing levies ranging from 10% to 41% and heightening concerns over their potential drag on the US economy.

data.title: Naver Earnings Jump as AI Push Boosts Growth — Update
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun

Naver reported solid second-quarter earnings as the South Korean internet giant continued its artificial-intelligence push to upgrade its core platform businesses.

Growth remained strong at the company's search-platform, commerce, fintech and content segments, Naver said Friday, as new AI-powered services boosted user engagement as well as its online advertising and other businesses.

The company "is focusing on strengthening its platform competitiveness and expanding monetization opportunities by incorporating AI technology," Chief Executive Choi Soo-yeon told an earning call, noting the growing importance of data globally.

Choi said Naver's "AI Briefing" feature, recently added to its search platform, led to an increased volume of business queries, more user visits and longer time spent online--all in support of advertising growth. The company plans to unveil an "AI Tab" in 2026 to further enhance the user experience, she said.

Naver's April-June net profit jumped 50% from a year earlier to 497.4 billion won, equivalent to $359.5 million, exceeding the 435.49 billion won consensus estimate of analysts in a FactSet poll.

Revenue climbed 12% to 2.915 trillion won, and operating profit rose 10% to 521.6 billion won.

The company attributed the solid quarterly earnings partly to its continued AI-powered advertising optimization and its integration of diverse content and data with AI for users.

Having developed the family of　homegrown AI models known as HyperCLOVA X, Naver is seen as better positioned than its local peers to benefit from the new Seoul government's "sovereign AI" initiative to invest in South Korean AI infrastructure, according to analysts.

Choi said Friday that she saw "significant new business opportunities" for Naver in building out AI data centers in South Korea and helping establish large language models for other countries in the Middle East and Southeast Asia.

Naver said its AI tool and relevant capability continued generating profit in the second quarter. The company is also pushing for more business-to-business deals in South Korea and overseas following its contract wins from the country's central bank and a state power utility to provide cloud-computing services.

Naver has also expanded its e-commerce business globally. The company said earlier this week that it will take an additional stake in Wallapop to fully acquire the Spanish consumer-to-consumer platform, strengthening its foothold in Europe's online-shopping sector.

The transaction followed Naver's purchase of Poshmark, a U.S. online marketplace, in a $1.2 billion deal that was finalized in early 2023.

Choi said she expects the Wallapop acquisition to help Naver secure diverse long-tail commerce data on consumer preferences and demand in Europe, as well as further growth in its consumer-to-consumer business.

Shares of Naver were recently 1.7% lower on Friday, trimming year-to-date gains to about 16%.

Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com

data.title: Australia 10-Year Yield Steady Ahead of RBA Decision
data.description:
Australia’s 10-year government bond yield held steady at 4.25% as investors shift their focus to the Reserve Bank's policy decision next week.

The central bank is widely expected to cut its cash rate by 25bps, after opting to hold rates last month in rare dissent.

Rate-cut expectations have been reinforced by last month’s weak inflation report, which showed that underlying inflation is gradually moving toward the RBA’s 2.5% target, alongside soft jobs data that revealed the first signs of strain in what had been an unusually resilient labor market.

In the latest economic news, Australia’s trade surplus in goods widened sharply in June, beating forecasts, driven mainly a rebound in exports while imports declined.

While the data signals some resilience in Australia’s external sector, it had little impact on monetary policy expectations.

Meanwhile, in the US, traders are ramping up bets on the Federal Reserve cutting rates this year, amid mounting concerns about the economic outlook.

data.title: Australia's Capricorn Metals gains tracking higher gold prices
data.description:
** Shares of gold miner Capricorn Metals rise as much as 1.6% to A$9.950

** U.S. gold futures for December delivery hit an all-time high of $3,534.10 following a report that the United States has imposed tariffs on imports of one-kilo gold bars

** Spot gold hit its highest since July 23

** Meanwhile, Jarden trims price target to A$9.64 from A$9.75, maintains 'overweight' rating on the stock

** Says marginal PT cut due to timing rather than fundamental changes, also sees lower FY27 production at Mt Gibson Gold Project (MGGP)

** Retains 'overweight' rating on upbeat gold prices and FY26 production expectations in the near term, and on likelihood of further mine plan optimization (underground inclusion) at MGGP

** 7 analysts rated 'buy' on avg; median PT is A$10.05, per LSEG-compiled data

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0338 GMT - DBS's strong performance has likely been priced in, Morningstar's Michael Makdad says in a note. DBS continued to deliver higher returns than its peers, with an annualized return on equity of around 17% in the second quarter versus 13% for OCBC and UOB, he says. Singapore's largest lender has also proven more resilient in the mid-2025 rate drop. The group was able to capitalize more effectively on a surge of liquidity to Asian financial hubs in early-May amid tariff tensions, he adds. "While DBS's performance merits a premium, we see relatively greater value in its peers at current levels." Makdad maintains his earnings forecasts for DBS and S$48.00 fair value estimate on the stock. Shares gain 1.9% to S$50.68. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0323 GMT - Taiwan chip makers TSMC and GlobalWafers should be exempt from the U.S.'s 100% semiconductor tariff, given their U.S. operations and ongoing expansion plans, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a research note. This could position TSMC and GWC more favorably among major U.S. customers, offering better cost advantages, they say. Meanwhile, as investors have been concerned about tariff uncertainties for months, the announcement helps remove valuation overhangs, they add. TSMC's share price has underperformed other AI names such as SK Hynix and Nvidia, partly due to geopolitical uncertainties, they say. The 100% tariff could also make TSMC's mature node's pricing--previously considered to be at a premium compared to peers--more competitive, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0312 GMT - Kakao's earnings growth momentum appears weak despite its stronger-than-expected 2Q results, Nomura analysts Angela Hong and Won Kang write in a note. The South Korean mobile internet company's solid 2Q profit margin was largely driven by reduced marketing expenses and other cost savings, they say. Operating profit in its core advertising and commerce segments remained flat on quarter and fell 17% on year in 2Q, they note. Nomura raises the target price by 23% to 59,000 won to reflect the 2Q margin improvement and the 2H launch of OpenAI-powered services but maintains a neutral rating on the stock. Shares are 0.9% higher at 64,200 won. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0259 GMT - Parkway Life REIT's near-term catalysts are on the horizon, OCBC Investment Research's Ada Lim says in a research report. Major works at the REIT's Mount Elizabeth Hospital property were completed at end-July, which should drive a substantial uplift in its FY 2026 distribution per unit, the analyst notes. Management is also in discussions with the REIT's sponsor to conduct asset enhancement initiatives at other Singapore hospitals, particularly Gleneagles Hospital. Moreover, the REIT is pursuing tax exemptions on foreign-sourced interest income for four of its 11 French properties, after obtaining similar approval from Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore for seven of them. OCBC raises the unit's fair value estimate to S$4.85 from S$4.65, with an unchanged buy rating. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0222 GMT - SD Guthrie is expected to post stronger 2H earnings, backed by gains from planned land sales and lower production costs, say CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia. The company is sticking to its MYR500 million-MYR700 million land sale target for 2025 and expects higher output and cheaper fertiliser to help cut average costs, they note. While cautious of rising labor costs, SD Guthrie believes the impact will be limited due to its higher level of automation, they say. They raise SD Guthrie 2025-2026 earnings forecast by 4%-11% to reflect higher palm kernel price assumptions. CIMB upgrades the company's rating to buy from hold and raises target price to MYR5.15 from MYR5.06. Shares are 1.5% higher at MYR4.84.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0222 GMT - Suntec REIT's rerating catalysts are in sight, DBS Group Research analysts say in a report. These include its strong underlying earnings in 1H, driven by healthy positive rental renewals, the analysts say. Also, with interest rates expected to continue falling, the REIT will likely benefit from further savings on borrowing costs. The analysts estimate that every 50 bps of savings in financing costs would lead to a roughly 10% upside to the REIT's distribution per unit. A third catalyst is the potential reinstatement of the managed investment trust structure in Australia. DBS upgrades the REIT to buy from hold and raises the target price to S$1.40 from S$1.15. Units are 0.8% lower at S$1.23. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0217 GMT - DBS keeps its bull at RHB Research after the Singapore lender delivered solid 2Q earnings. DBS's hedges and balance sheet growth helped the bank post more resilient figures than its peers, RHB's Singapore Research team says in a note. The Singapore bank says its S$0.24-a-share increase in annual dividends should be sustainable for 2026, which reaffirms RHB's thesis for capital returns and dividend commitments. Nonetheless, the analysts reckon DBS will report softer 2H numbers due to seasonality and U.S. tariffs. RHB raises its target price for DBS to S$52.80 from S$47.00 to reflect lower cost of equity expectations after DBS's strong earnings, and maintains its buy rating. Shares are 2.1% higher at S$50.80. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0210 GMT - Mr. D.I.Y. Group (M) is expected to report a sequentially lower 2Q net profit of MYR150 million-MYR160 million due to post-festive seasonality, RHB IB anlayst Soong Wei Siang says in a note. However, gross profit margins should remain strong, thanks to favorable FX movements and an increasing scale of operations, he says. With inflation pressuring consumers to bargain-hunt, Mr D.I.Y. is well-positioned to benefit from its wide store network, affordable products and strong brand, he notes. Its tie-up with Chinese retailer KK Group also helps diversify offerings to attract younger and female consumers, he adds. Soong believes generous dividends will continue, supported by solid cash flow. RHB maintains a buy rating on Mr. D.I.Y. Group with an unchanged target price at MYR1.87. Shares are 1.2% lower at MYR1.56. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0158 GMT - Liontown is unlikely to generate positive cashflow until FY 2028, according to Citi analyst Kate McCutcheon. That is based on Citi's lithium-price forecasts, which average around US$830/metric ton through the end of next year. "While LTR is a quality orebody with expandable scale, we keep our sell rating on valuation grounds," she says in a note. Citi's target on Liontown is cut to A$0.40 from A$0.50 previously. Liontown shares are halted. The stock last traded at A$0.845/share. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0150 GMT - The proposed 100% U.S. tariff on chips will hurt the Malaysian tech sector, and uncertainty over tariffs could delay investment and expansion in Malaysia, especially for companies reliant on U.S. demand,CIMB Securities analyst Mohd Shanaz Noor Azam says in a note. That is despite short-term gains for the sector due to increased demand before the tariffs take effect, he says. Automated test equipment makers like Greatech Technology and Genetec Technology, with high U.S. exposure, are most at risk, he says. Outsourced semiconductor assembly and testing players such as Unisem (M) and Malaysian Pacific Industries are more insulated as they have fewer direct U.S. shipments, he adds. CIMB names Inari Amertron and Malaysian Pacific Industries as its top picks in the sector. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0118 GMT - ASX's bears at UBS think that a regulatory inquiry into the market operator will lead to a recurring increase in its operating costs. ASX's warning of a A$30 million hit from the Australian Securities and Investment Commission's inquiry is seen by UBS analysts as implying a one-off 4% impact to fiscal 2026 EPS. However, they anticipate another A$20 million in fiscal 2027 and also see elevated cost growth the following year. Cost growth is likely to outstrip revenue growth in fiscal 2027 and fiscal 2028, they warn clients in a note. UBS cuts its target price 7.9% to A$63.65 and keeps a sell rating on the stock, which is up 0.9% at A$64.80. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0112 GMT - SD Guthrie is expected to book over MYR500 million in land disposal gains in 2H, providing a key lift to its earnings as palm oil prices ease, Maybank IB analyst Ong Chee Ting says in a note. He expects weaker 2H plantation earnings to be weaker than 1H, mainly due to lower average selling prices. The planter is maintaining its full-year output growth target of 3%-5%, with improved harvests in the pipeline, he notes. Ong raises SD Guthrie's 2025 core earnings forecast by 6% citing a higher upstream contribution and sees further upside if land deals are finalized. Maybank raises SD Guthrie's target price to MYR5.64 from MYR5.52, while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are unchanged at MYR4.77.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)
